title: Am Rhein. - No. III. (Index) - Nonnenwerth.
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He shtood peside de Kloster-place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oopon de Rheinisch shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und dere he saw a lofely face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d seen in treams pefore.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Feinslieb, und willן¿½st dou go mit me?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Feinsllieb, make no delay;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For rocks ish shdeep und vales ish teep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und dings ish in de way.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Und oh! how can I go mit dir,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or flyen out of land?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der bischof holts me py de law,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Rheingraf by der hand.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Liebsherz, if dou couldן¿½st landwarts gehn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½d follow willingly;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boot we are leafs, und shdrongן¿½s de shdem
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vitch pinds oos to de dree.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Der briest who helt dee py de law
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ish now a broken man;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Rheingraf who vouldt marry dee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ish in der Kaisarן¿½s ban.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Und if de Kloster-beoples here
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vill shdop your goin to town,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bei Gott! Iן¿½ll burn von half of dem,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De oder half Iן¿½ll trown!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Denn linger not to back dy drunk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boot led our lofe hafe vings;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dereן¿½s milliners in fair Cologne,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vill make you avery dings.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She toorn her eyes im mondenschein,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She schmile so heafenly;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dear lofe, so shendle und so goot!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll cut away mit dee.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Und do not kill de Kloster-volk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tvouldt only bring tiscrace!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dough if I had de abbess here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lort! how Iן¿½d slap her vace!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De moonlighdt blayed oopon de drees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It shined oopon de blain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two forms rode in de mitnight woods,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und nefer coomed again.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Charles G. Leland represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Charles G. Leland's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
